title-group.article-title: Pyogranulomatous dermatitis with Enterococcus faecalis in a spotted seal (Phoca larga)

author-notes.corresp: *CorrespondenceHongxuan He, Institute of Zoology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100101, China.Email: hehx@ioz.ac.cn

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2022 The Authors. Veterinary Medicine and Science published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/ License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited and is not used for commercial purposes.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Cutaneous infections are important diseases in captive and free‐ranging pinnipeds and are associated with various causative agents. Some special agents, such as fungi, morbillivirus and mycobacteria, can cause cutaneous specific granulomatous inflammation.

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: To identity the cause of chronic dermatitis in a spotted seal in an aquarium.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Herein, we analyze the clinical history and cutaneous samples of the spotted seal through differential diagnosis (histopathology, microorganism culture, special histochemical staining methods, PCR), and antibiotic susceptibility test.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: This is a rare pyogranulomatous dermatitis case caused by E. faecalis in a captive adult male spotted seal (Phoca largha) in an aquarium.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: We provide a meaningful approach to the diagnosis and treatment of bacterial dermatitis in pinnipeds.

abstract.p: A rare pyogranulomatous dermatitis case caused by E. faecalis in a captive adult male spotted seal (Phoca largha) was described. We provide a meaningful approach to the diagnosis and treatment of bacterial dermatitis in pinnipeds.

award-group.funding-source: Youth Joint Innovation Project of Shaanxi Academy of Sciences

p.mixed-citation: Wu, Q., Wang, Y., Zhang, X., Gu, X., He, H., & Jin, X. (2023). Pyogranulomatous dermatitis with Enterococcus faecalis in a spotted seal (Phoca larga). Veterinary Medicine and Science, 9, 985–991. 10.1002/vms3.973PMC1002988036253881

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Spotted seals (Phoca largha), (order: Carnivora, suborder: Pinniped family: Phocidae), are important and ornate aquarium animals. Cutaneous lesions are a common issue in captive and free‐ranging pinnipeds and are associated with several causative agents, such as fungi, viruses, parasites and bacteria (Eo & Kwon, 2014; Lipscomb et al., 2001; Manire et al., 2006; Pollock et al., 2000; van Beurden et al., 2015). Several severe dermatitis cases caused by different agents have been reported in pinnipeds, including Fusarium sp. And Malassezia spp. in South American sea lions (Ahn et al., 2014; Reisfeld et al., 2019), Candida albicans in spotted seals (Eo & Kwon, 2014), Morbillivirus and Pox virus in seals and sea lions (Guardo et al., 2005; Nollens, 2005), Anisakis spp. in the bottlenose dolphin, Tursiops truncatus and Phocoena phocoena in harbour porpois e (van Beurden et al., 2015), Erysipelothrix rhusiopathiae in harbour seals (Opriessnig et al., 2013) and Mycobacterium fortuitum in southern sea lions (Lewis, 1987).

Enterococcus faecalis (E. faecalis) formerly classified as part of the Group D Streptococcus system is a Gram‐positive bacterium and one of the major commensal bacteria in the gastrointestinal tracts of humans and other mammals (Murray et al., 2021). E. faecalis is also an opportunistic pathogen that has been reported in the cases in humans and domestic animals, including orchitis, septicaemia, mastitis, endocarditis, urethritis, etc. (Chadfield et al., 2004; Gupta et al., 2005; Jiang et al., 2011; Leeson et al., 2021; Výrostková et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2019). In occasional cases, E. faecalis can cause skin infection in atopic dermatitis and perianal dermatitis in humans (Malara et al., 2020; Theotokatou et al., 2014). Herein, we report a rare case of pyogranulomatous dermatitis caused by E. faecalis in a spotted seal.

sec.title: Animal information
sec.p: A captive‐born adult (10 years old) male spotted seal weighing 105 kg, transferred from another aquarium in 2013, was kept together with 9 other spotted seals in an indoor exhibit, a 500 m3 saltwater swimming pool with resting areas (artificial sandy beach and rocks), kept at approximately 2.8%–3.1% salinity, water temperature ranging from 11.6 to 30.7°C with the seasons, pH ranging from 6.99 to 8.12 and residual chlorine ranging from 0.1 to 0.5 ppm. The pool was equipped with water circulation and ultraviolet sterilization systems, solid and biological filters and ozone. These animals were fed daily with a varied fish‐based diet (Cynoglossus joyneri, Pseudosciaena polyactis, Sebastes schlegeli and Hexagrammos otakii) and were supplemented daily with multivitamins (vitamin A, vitamin D, vitamin E, vitamin C and B vitamins) for pinnipeds.

sec.title: Clinical history
sec.p: In October 2018, two cutaneous lesions of 8×8 cm in diameter were found on this spotted seal's back (front of right foreflipper) during a routine physical examination. The lesions were swollen and warm. Hence subcutaneous injection was immediately initiated with cefovecin sodium (Zoetis) 8 mg/kg for two treatment courses (half a month apart). The lesions were visually monitored daily. In January 2019, the infected areas gradually softened with pus draining. The treatments included metronidazole (Venturepharm Group Inc.) 10 mg/kg PO QID and potassium amoxicillin‐clavulanate (Synulox, Zoetis) 3 mg/kg PO TID for 14 days, subcutaneous injection of cefovecin sodium 8 mg/kg for three treatment courses and washing the lesions with 3% hydrogen peroxide solution. A similar therapeutic schedule was applied when the lesions were ruptured in May and August. In November 2019, the area of the lesion expanded to 25 × 20 cm in diameter (Figure 1a) and subcutaneous fat changed gradually from liquefaction to caseation and the lesion was still developing in‐depth by B ultrasound examination. Unfortunately, both microbial culture and antimicrobial susceptibility tests were not performed during the treatment.

caption.p: Gross morphology of the infectious skin. (a) The size of fusion cutaneous lesion is approximately 25 × 20 cm in diameter. (b) Multiple cream‐like suppurative zones varying in size are distributed in the middle area of the dermis (arrow)

sec.p: In May 2020, surgery removed the cutaneous lesions due to an increased risk of septicaemia. Before the surgery, blood samples were collected for haematology and serum chemistry examination. The abnormal results included a high white blood cell count (WBC, 53.67 × 109/L, reference range 6.21–15.97 × 109/L) and a high neutrophil percentage (88.8%, reference range 33%–74%) (Han et al., 2018) (S1). The surgery was performed in the sterile operating room in the animal hospital. This animal was under inhalation anaesthesia with Isoflurane (RWD Life Science Co., LTD), and the infectious skin was removed. Then, the removed skin tissues were immediately transferred to a clean bench in a sterile container, washed with sterile saline solution to drop the residual iodophor and ethanol and divided into small pieces of samples. Partial skin samples were inoculated on nutrient agar and Sabouraud's agar medium (SDA) for bacterial and fungal cultures, respectively. Another sample was stored at –20°C and fixed in 4% neutral paraformaldehyde respectively. At the same time, the fresh samples stored at 4°C were also sent to the First Affiliated Hospital of Xi'an Jiaotong University (FAHXJTU) for bacterial identification and antibiotic susceptibility testing that referenced the CLSI (Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute) M100 (CLSI, 2020).

sec.title: Pathological diagnosis
sec.p: The cutaneous samples appeared pale and had a tough texture. Multiple cream‐like suppurative zones varying in size were distributed in the middle area of the dermis (Figure 1b). The border between the suppurative zone and the healthy zone was not clear.

The fixed samples were processed into paraffin sections and stained with haematoxylin‐eosin, toluidine blue (TB), Mallory trichrome, periodic acid Schiff (PAS) and acid‐fast stains for pathological examination (Chang et al., 2021; Di Marco Lo Presti et al., 2022; Seifi et al., 2012). Histopathologically, the structure of the epidermis and the papillary layer was clear and intact. The primary lesion was mainly located in the deep reticular layer of the dermis and its boundary was not clear (Figure 2a). The lesion was constructed of numerous oval masses of epithelioid cells. Dead neutrophils were in the centre of these masses, and the epithelioid cells phagocytised the neutrophils and cocci (Figure 2b). Numerous lymphocytes, plasma cells, capillaries and a few multinucleated giant cells were observed around the masses of epithelioid cells (Figure 2c). Liquefaction necrosis (purulency) was located in the margins of the lesions (Figure 2d). In addition, some small lymphocytes infiltration foci (Figure 2a) were distributed in the deeper dermis. TB staining showed numerous mast cells distributed in infectious foci, lymphocytic foci and blood capillaries (Figure 3a). Fibre proliferated mildly in infected foci (Figure 3b).These pathological changes showed that the cutaneous lesion was a chronic granulomatous inflammation that was usually caused by fungi and mycobacteria. Therefore, PAS and acid‐fast staining were applied, and no fungal hyphae or acid‐fast bacteria were found (Figure 3c and d).

caption.p: Histopathological changes in cutaneous lesions. (a) The structure of the epidermis and the papillary layer is clear and intact. The primary lesion is mainly located in the deep reticular layer of the dermis and its boundary is not clear. Some lymphocyte foci are distributed in the deeper dermis (arrow). E: the epidermis layer; L: the lesions in dermis. (b) The lesion is constructed of numerous oval masses of epithelioid cells. Dead neutrophils are in the centre of these masses, and the epithelioid cells phagocytised the neutrophils and cocci (The magnified image at the upper right corner). (c) Numerous lymphocytes, plasma cells, capillaries and a few multinucleated giant cells (M) are observed around the masses of epithelioid cells. (d) Liquefaction necrosis (purulency) is located in the margins of the lesions (S). Captured by 3DHISTECH PANNORAMIC 250 Flash III DX and SlideViewer Ver 2.5

Histochemical staining of skin lesions. (a) Numerous mast cells are located in suppurative foci. TB stain. (b) Fibre proliferated mildly in infected foci. L: infected foci; Ds: the fibrous tissue of the dermis. Mallory trichrome stain. (c) No fungal hyphae are observed in the infected area. PAS stain. (d) There is no fluorescent signal of acid‐fast bacteria in the infected area. Acid‐fast (fluorescence) stain. a, b and c are captured by Olympus CX43 microscope and EPview Ver 1.2. d is captured by Olympus BX53 fluorescence microscope and cellSens standard Ver 3.1

sec.title: Pathogen identification
sec.p: Corresponding to the granulomatous inflammation, several suspicious infectious agents were detected by microorganism culture and PCR. First, fungi were common agents in pinnipeds, but no fungi grew on the SDA agar after 48 h of culturing. On the nutrition agar, small white, smooth‐surface colonies grew. The bacterium was a Gram‐positive cocci arranged in chains or clumps observed on Gram stained under an optical microscope (Figure 4). The cultured bacterium and infectious cutaneous sample were identified by PCR targeting of the bacterial 16S ribosomal RNA gene (16S rRNA) (S2) (Woo et al., 2008). A 777 bp sequence (GenBank accession: OK135605) was obtained from the cultured bacteria and infected cutaneous sample. Alignment analysis determined that the 16S rRNA gene in this study shared a high identity (from 99.5% to 99.7%) with that of other E. faecalis strains, but shared an 83.6% identity with the Streptococcus pyogenes strain (NR028598.1) (Figure 5a). Furthermore, using the Streptococcus pyogenes 16S rRNA sequence as an outgroup, a phylogenetic tree was established. It showed that the bacterial isolate was related to the ten isolates of E. faecalis in GenBank (Figure 5b).

caption.p: Bacteria isolated from infected skin samples. Bacteria are Gram‐positive cocci that arrange in chains or clumps. The magnification is in the upper right corner. A single Gram‐positive coccus (arrowhead). Gram stain. Captured by Olympus CX43 microscope and EPview Ver 1.2

Alignment and phylogenetic tree based on the 16S rRNA gene sequences. (a) Alignment analysis. The 16S rRNA gene in this study shares a high identity (from 99.5% to 99.7%) with that of other E. faecalis strains but shares an 83.6% identity with the Streptococcus pyogenes strain (NR028598.1). (b) Phylogenetic tree. The Streptococcus pyogenes 16S rRNA sequence is used as an outgroup. The phylogenetic tree shows that the bacterial isolate is related to 10 other isolates of E. faecalis in GenBank; *the E. faecalis isolate in the case. The phylogenetic tree is constructed with MEGA‐7 software

sec.p: Second, the DNA and RNA of frozen cutaneous samples were extracted for PCR amplification to further detect the 18S ribosomal RNA gene (18S rRNA) of fungi, the B2L gene of poxvirus and the phosphoprotein gene of morbillivirus. The detection methods of poxvirus and morbillivirus are shown in S2 (Chang et al., 2021; Felipe‐Jiménez et al., 2022; Nollens, 2005). These results were negative.

In addition, the bacterial identification result was also consistent with FAHXJTU. The results of the antibiotic susceptibility test using the minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) method using broth microdilution from FAHXJTU are shown in table following the guidelines of CLSI. The isolate of E. faecalis was only resistant to the erythromycin, and sensitive to other antibiotics in the test.

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: In the present case, the spotted seal had a chronic cutaneous lesions according to the clinical history. Although various antibiotics were applied for treatment, the lesions were relapsing and uncontrolled. During the treatment, microorganism culture and antibiotic susceptibility tests were not performed until the surgery, and it was a serious mistake in regard to the treatments of infection. Therefore, we cannot also know what the original infection is.

Nevertheless, E. faecalis was the only microorganism isolated from the removed cutaneous samples. Although E. faecalis is an important causative agent in humans and animals, dermatitis caused by E. faecalis is rare. In previous reports, E. faecalis can cause perianal dermatitis in children and skin infection in atopic dermatitis (Malara et al., 2020; Theotokatou et al., 2014). Histopathological examination showed that the lesions were very deep and wide in the dermis and that the epidermis was not affected. Mast cells and fibrous proliferation can reflect the degree and progression of inflammation (Zachary, 2017). In the lesions, many mast cells infiltrated. Mast cells are important components of the innate immune system and are mainly distributed in connective tissues throughout the body. Upon bacterial exposure, they are recruited and release a wide variety of cytokines and chemokines to have immunoregulatory functions and suppress chronic inflammation, including IL‐2, IL‐10, IL‐17, IL‐18 and TGF‐β, by degranulation or the canonical secretory pathway (Melissa et al., 2016; Zhang & Kurashima, 2021). Furthermore, mast cells may contribute to late fibrosis in infectious granuloma by proteases and TGF‐β (Garcia‐Rodriguez et al., 2021). Connective tissue proliferation is a common sign of chronic inflammation and it can limit the expansion of infection, such as forming a fibrous envelope (Zachary, 2017). In this case, the connective tissue proliferation was mild in lesions. Thus, the mild connective tissue proliferation may be conducive to the infection expansion.

In previous reports, the common causative agents of cutaneous granuloma are diverse in animals (Imadojemu & Rosenbach, 2019; Thomas et al., 2018). Therefore, the differential diagnosis is important and suspicious agents include fungi, poxvirus, morbillivirus, parasites, mycobacteria, Listeria, Staphylococcus and Streptococcus (Díaz‐Delgado et al., 2017). First, fungi are the common causative agents in pinnipeds and form many hyphae that can be stained by PAS in the infected area, and the 18S rRNA gene is the usual target for fungal detection (Chang et al., 2021). All results of fungal culture, morphology and molecular biology examination were negative in this case. Second, there were no viral inclusions found in lesions, and the poxvirus and mobillivirus target genes were also negative in this case. Third, the bodies or eggs of parasites are easy to observe in cases of cutaneous lesions infected with parasites and can also be stained by PAS (van Beurden et al., 2015). In this case, no bodies or eggs of parasites were found in the cutaneous lesions. Therefore, fungal, viral and parasitic infections were all excluded. Finally, bacteria were identified by both us and FAHXJTU using the skin samples collected aseptically. Only one kind of bacterium grew in the agar, and it was identified as E. faecalis by 16S rRNA gene alignment. 16S rRNA detection is a common method of bacterial identification and detection from infectious tissues (Travis et al., 2020). The E. faecalis 16s rRNA gene fragment was also directly detected from the infectious cutaneous sample without other bacteria. Meanwhile, the acid‐fast bacteria, including mycobacteria, were excluded through negative acid‐fast staining in cutaneous samples.

Therefore, it was doubtless that pyogranulomatous inflammation was associated with E. faecalis in this captive spotted seal. The granuloma formation was mainly because causative agents are not degraded completely which stimulate the tissue reaction. In the lesions, the residual bodies of bacteria in the cytoplasm of epithelioid cells were found. Epithelioid cells are the hyperphagocytic macrophages (Zachary, 2017). The mechanism by which E. faecalis causes cutaneous granuloma may be similar to that of other intracellular bacteria (Orme & Basaraba, 2014).

E. faecalis infection is difficult to treat, mainly because of the existence of multiple antibiotic‐resistant strains (Lins et al., 2013). However, according to the antibiotic susceptibility test, this E. faecalis isolate was sensitive to many common antibiotics in this case.

Therefore, the reasons why the cutaneous lesions continuously expanded after treatment with multiple antibiotics were as follows: 1. The course of antibiotic treatment was not enough. In the clinical history, the antibiotic treatment was usually 1–2 courses, and the treatment stopped when the cutaneous lesions were better. This did not guarantee that the infection was completely eliminated. 2. The dose of antibiotics was not enough. The pharmacokinetics of antibiotics in different animals are different (Toutain et al., 2010). This may lead to low blood concentrations and influence the effect of antibiotics in spotted seals. Moreover, because microorganism isolation and the antibiotic susceptibility test were deficient in the early stages of infection, the choice and usage of antibiotics were insufficiently accurate.

In conclusion, we diagnosed the first case of pyogranulomatous dermatitis associated with E. faecalis infection in a captive spotted seal. It has important reference significance for the treatment of pinniped infections.

sec.title: AUTHOR CONTRIBUTIONS
sec.p: Qiaoxing Wu: project administration; writing – original draft. Yamian Wang: data curation; investigation. Xinke Zhang: data curation; investigation. Xiangyang Gu: writing – review & editing. Hongxuan He: funding acquisition; project administration; supervision; validation; writing – review & editing. Xuelin Jin: project administration; supervision.

sec.title: CONFLICT OF INTEREST
sec.p: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

sec.title: ETHICAL STATEMENT
sec.p: The authors confirm that the ethical policies of the journal, as noted on the journal's author guidelines page, have been adhered to. All relevant guidelines for the use of animals in scientific studies were followed. The study did not include any experimentation on animals or humans, and samples were taken from surgical treatment that was approved by animal owner.

sec.title: PEER REVIEW
sec.p: The peer review history for this article is available at https://publons.com/publon/10.1002/vms3.973.

ack.title: ACKNOWLEDGEMENT
ack.p: We thank professor Zhenxuan Liu at Taiwan University for pathological observation.

sec.title: DATA AVAILABILITY STATEMENT
sec.p: The data that support the findings of this study are openly available in GenBank at https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/nuccore/OK135605.1, reference number OK135605.1.
